Title: The antagonistic MYB paralogs RH1 and RH2 govern anthocyanin leaf markings in Medicago truncatula

Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Universally expressed in the three different leaf regions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Universally expressed in the three different leaf regions
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf markings, especially red border region of leaflets
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for RH1- and RH2-mediated control of anthocyanin leaf markings; loss reduces anthocyanin pigmentation, producing pale green spots and suppressing RH1-driven red markings
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MtTT8 physically interacts with both RH1 and RH2. 2. MtTT8 is required for RH1-mediated anthocyanin leaf marking formation, as rh1d mttt8 loses red pigmentation and shows pale green spots. 3. MtTT8 is also required for RH2-mediated control because rh2 mttt8 resembles mttt8.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tnt1 mutant analysis; double-mutant genetic analysis with rh1d, rh1 and rh2; Y2H; BiFC; co-immunoprecipitation; transient luciferase assay with MtANS promoter
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plants grown under 24°C:22°C day:night with 16 h:8 h photoperiod
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts as a cofactor in the MBW complex rather than the pattern-determining component; its broad expression suggests spatial patterning is conferred mainly by RH1 and RH2.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: mttt8 plants showed reduced anthocyanin pigmentation in whole leaflets and petioles

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: WD40 repeat protein, WDR family member
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Universally expressed in the three different leaf regions
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Universally expressed in the three different leaf regions
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf markings, especially red border region of leaflets
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Required for RH1- and RH2-mediated anthocyanin leaf marking formation; loss severely weakens markings and suppresses RH1-driven pigmentation
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. MtWD40-1 physically interacts with both RH1 and RH2. 2. mtwd40-1 mutants show faint leaf markings and reduced anthocyanin pigmentation. 3. Double-mutant analysis demonstrates MtWD40-1 is required for RH1- and RH2-mediated control of anthocyanin leaf markings.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tnt1 mutant analysis; double-mutant genetic analysis with rh1d, rh1 and rh2; Y2H; BiFC; co-immunoprecipitation; transient luciferase assay with MtANS promoter
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plants grown under 24°C:22°C day:night with 16 h:8 h photoperiod
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Broadly expressed MBW complex component; likely contributes permissively rather than spatially specifying the pattern.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: mtwd40-1 plants showed strongly weakened leaf markings and less anthocyanin pigmentation in leaflets and petioles

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Subgroup 6 R2R3-MYB; contains R2 and R3 DNA-binding domains, bHLH-interacting motif, ANDV motif, and [R/K]Px[P/A/R]xx[F/Y] motif; C-terminal activation domain required for activity
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf markings, particularly the red border on adaxial basal leaflet region
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Anthocyanin biosynthesis and patterned anthocyanin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation; direct promoter binding; positive autoregulation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: MtCHS; MtANS; MtF3H; MtF3'H; MtDFR1; MtDFR2; RH1
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates MtCHS and MtANS directly; positively upregulates MtF3H, MtF3'H, MtDFR1, MtDFR2 and its own promoter
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MtTT8; MtWD40-1; RH2 (functional competition in MBW context)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Functions in MBW complex with MtTT8 and MtWD40-1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Central activator and decisive positive regulator of anthocyanin leaf markings
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Predominantly expressed in leaf markings, particularly in the red border
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Predominantly expressed in leaf markings, particularly in the red border
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes anthocyanin leaf marking formation; upregulation enhances and enlarges red leaf spots, while loss of function abolishes leaf anthocyanin markings
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RH1 was identified as the key regulator underlying anthocyanin leaf markings in M. truncatula. 2. RH1 is a subgroup 6 R2R3-MYB activator whose C-terminal domain confers transactivation activity. 3. RH1 directly binds and activates anthocyanin biosynthetic gene promoters and also activates its own promoter. 4. RH1 requires MtTT8 and MtWD40-1 to function effectively in the MBW complex. 5. Loss of RH1 abolishes anthocyanin leaf markings, whereas RH1 upregulation or overexpression enhances anthocyanin accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Map-based cloning; BSA-seq; RNA-seq; qRT-PCR; CRISPR/Cas9 knockout in rh1d; Tnt1 insertion mutants; yeast transactivation assay; Arabidopsis protoplast dual-luciferase assay; DNA-binding assay; EMSA; overexpression in M. truncatula; genetic analysis with mttt8 and mtwd40-1
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plants grown under 24°C:22°C day:night with 16 h:8 h photoperiod
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Genomic deletion in rh1d causes RH1 upregulation; RH2 antagonizes RH1 activity and expression
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Positive autoregulation of RH1 expression; RH2-mediated antagonism dampens this positive feedback
Regulation_Genes.Epigenetic_Regulation: Possible but not proven; discussion suggests RH1 upregulation in rh1d may involve chromatin modifications caused by the 215 kb deletion and transposable elements
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression causes ectopic anthocyanin accumulation and enlarged/enhanced leaf spots; possible influence on chlorophyll/carotenoid patterning was suggested but not validated
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: BSA-seq (Illumina Hi-Seq 2500; accession NA); RNA-seq (Illumina NovaSeq, three biological replicates; accession NA)
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S promoter (constitutive, overexpression/transient assays); native RH1 promoter (endogenous autoregulation); U6 promoter (for CRISPR gRNA expression)

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Paralogous R2R3-MYB related to subgroup 6 anthocyanin MYBs; retains R2/R3 domains and interaction motifs but has a short divergent inactive C-terminal domain
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaf markings, especially yellow spot region where RH2 transcript is highest
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Negative modulation of anthocyanin biosynthesis and patterned anthocyanin accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Passive antagonistic competition within MBW complex; competition for promoter binding and shared cofactors; indirect repression of RH1 autoregulation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: RH1; anthocyanin biosynthetic gene promoters including MtANS and MtCHS (competitive binding context)
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Suppresses RH1-mediated activation of MtANS and RH1 promoter activity; indirectly lowers expression of anthocyanin biosynthetic genes by antagonizing RH1
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: MtTT8; MtWD40-1; RH1 (functional antagonism in MBW context)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts in the MBW complex as a competitor of RH1 through interaction with MtTT8 and MtWD40-1
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Antagonistic paralog functioning as a molecular rheostat that restrains RH1-dependent anthocyanin accumulation in leaf markings
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in leaf markings, with highest transcript in yellow spots
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Highly expressed in leaf markings, with highest transcript in yellow spots
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Negatively regulates anthocyanin leaf markings; loss of RH2 enhances anthocyanin spots, whereas RH2 overexpression represses RH1-driven anthocyanin markings and reduces RH1 transcript abundance
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. RH2 is a paralog of RH1 but lacks transcriptional activation activity because of its divergent C-terminal domain. 2. RH2 still binds the same promoter cis-elements and interacts with the same MBW partners, MtTT8 and MtWD40-1. 3. RH2 acts as an antagonist/competitor of RH1, reducing activation of anthocyanin biosynthetic genes and RH1 itself. 4. rh2 loss-of-function mutants show enhanced anthocyanin leaf spots, while RH2 overexpression suppresses the rh1d phenotype. 5. RH2 therefore fine-tunes patterned pigmentation by multidimensionally antagonizing RH1.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Tnt1 knockout mutants; qRT-PCR; yeast transactivation assay; domain swapping; Arabidopsis protoplast dual-luciferase assay; DNA-binding assay; EMSA; Y2H; BiFC; co-immunoprecipitation; overexpression in rh1d; genetic crosses and double-mutant analysis
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Plants grown under 24°C:22°C day:night with 16 h:8 h photoperiod
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Loss of RH2 in the 215 kb deletion contributes to increased RH1 expression in rh1d
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Antagonizes RH1 positive autoregulation, thereby reducing RH1 promoter activity and transcript abundance
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression represses anthocyanin leaf markings; no other obvious phenotypes reported for rh2 knockout
Regulation_Genes.Applied_Promoters: 35S promoter (constitutive, overexpression/transient assays); native RH2 promoter (reporter assay, not activated by RH1 or RH2)
